quotes.verbatim_quote:
In our western music system we have twelve tones that are repeated over and over span-
ning many octaves. We call this set of tones the chromatic scale. All of the notes in the
chromatic scale are one half step (one fret on the guitar) away from each other. Two half
steps would equal a whole step (two frets on the guitar).
quotes.why_load_bearing: This passage defines the chromatic scale and anchors half-step and whole-step intervals to physical fret distances on the guitar, establishing the foundational unit of measure for all subsequent theory.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
In our culture, most of our musical resources are derived from the major scale. A major
scale can begin on any one of the twelve tones found in the chromatic scale. The whole
step/half step formula for a major scale is:
quotes.why_load_bearing: This introduces the major scale as the root of virtually all concepts in the method and signals that its W-W-H-W-W-W-H intervallic formula is the generative rule from which chords, keys, and scales are built.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
To build a C Major scale, start with the note C. Now we move one whole step up to find
the next note, which is D. Another whole step will bring us to the note E. One half step
away from E is F. (Take a look at Example 3 and you will notice that there are no sharp or
flat notes between E and F or Band C.) Continuing, a whole step up from F is G, another
whole step up brings us to A, and yet another brings us to B. Our last move will be a half
step up from B to C. We have just constructed a C Major scale.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Walks the student step-by-step through applying the W-W-H-W-W-W-H formula to C, modeling the procedure they must replicate for all twelve keys.

quotes.topic: memorize all 12 major scales away from guitar
quotes.verbatim_quote: What you need to do now is construct all twelve major scales on paper, away from your guitar. Construct them in the following order*: C, F, B♭, E♭, A♭, D♭, G♭, B, E, A, D and G. Check your results against the example below and start memorizing them by recitation away from your instrument. The importance of knowing this cannot be over stressed. Nearly every theoretical concept will be based on this information, and the better you know these scales, the easier your musical studies will be.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Issues the method's central prerequisite rule: all twelve major scales must be memorized away from the instrument because every subsequent concept depends on them.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
In many styles of music, like rock, pop, country and jazz, chords tend to move in patterns.
One of the most common patterns in these styles is the movement of a 4th. The cycle (or
circle) of 4ths shows this movement. The twelve roots (of chords or keys) are shown
around the cycle. If you follow them counterclockwise each root is a 4th higher than the
one before it—hence the name cycle of 4ths. If you follow them clockwise each root is a
5th higher than the one before—hence the name cycle of 5ths.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the cycle of 4ths/5ths and explains its directional logic, establishing the navigational framework used to organize chord progressions and the ordering of all exercises throughout the book.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The area between the clef and the time
signature at the beginning of a tune is
called the key signature. The sharps or
flats found in the key signature are de-
rived from the major scale that is the
basis for the tune. The number of sharps
or flats, or their absence, therefore, will
tell you the key of the tune.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines key signatures and directly links them back to the major scales just memorized, teaching the student how to decode a tune's key from notation.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
When determining an interval’s name,
be sure to include both notes in your count through the musical alphabet, starting with
the bottom note and counting upward. For instance, the distance from C to F would be
counted like this: C-1, D-2, E-3, F-4. So, the interval from C to F is a 4th.
quotes.why_load_bearing: States the procedural rule for naming intervals—inclusive counting from the bottom note—which is the method students must use to correctly identify all intervals going forward.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
In this book you will be learning six different fingerings for the major scale. Three of these
fingerings will have their roots on the sixth string and three will have their roots on the
fifth string. We will identify these fingerings by what string the lowest root is found on and
with which finger that root is played. The first fingering below will be labeled 6/1 because
the lowest root is found on the sixth string and fingered with the first finger. The second
fingering will be called 5/1 because the lowest root is found on the fifth string and fingered
with the first finger.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Introduces the book's string/finger position-naming system (e.g., 6/1, 5/1) that labels all six major scale fingerings, essential for following every fingering reference throughout the method.

quotes.topic: practicing melodic patterns through scale fingerings
quotes.verbatim_quote:
After you have worked with these melodic patterns play all the major scale fingerings with
every melodic pattern you have learned. Play them forwards and backwards in all octaves
of the fingerings you know.
quotes.why_load_bearing: States the practice rule for combining melodic patterns with all six scale fingerings in both directions, defining the systematic drill approach the method prescribes.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Triads are built from the root, 3rd and 5th degrees of the major scale (R, 3, 5). These
notes can be altered in such a way as to give us four different types of triads: major, minor,
diminished and augmented.
quotes.why_load_bearing: States the foundational rule for triad construction and introduces the four triad qualities that will be systematically fingered across all string sets and inversions in the chapter.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
When the notes are sounded from bottom to top in root, 3rd, 5th order, it is referred to
as being in root position. When the notes are sounded in 3rd, 5th, root order, the term first
inversion is used. The order 5th, root, 3rd is called second inversion. No matter how you play
it, however, it is still a C Major triad.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines all three inversion names with their precise voicing orders, establishing the vocabulary used in every subsequent fingering diagram and etude.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
What we are going to do first is
divide the guitar into four string sets. For instance, considered together, the sixth, fifth

and fourth strings are a string set.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Establishes the four three-string groupings that organize every triad fingering in the chapter, making this the structural grid for all root-position, first-inversion, and second-inversion exercises.
